div.head: Sutra Passages (HDS 1, 2, 5, 6, 16.2, 22)
div.p:
The six sutra passages carved at Mount Hongding are taken from four different sutras, of which three belong to the group of scriptures dealing with the Perfection of Wisdom ( prajñāpāramitā , bore boluomi 般若波羅蜜). Three of the carved passages are, furthermore, quotations from the same sutra, namely the Sutra on the Great Perfection of Wisdom Spoken by Mañjuśrī
( Wenshushili suoshuo mohe bore boluomi jing
文殊師利所說摩訶般若波羅蜜經; T#232, 8: 726a-732c). Thematically, the sutra excerpts focus on Buddha contemplation (guanfo 觀佛), the concept of emptiness ( śūnyatā , kong 空), and the Six Perfections of a bodhisattva ( ṣaṭ­pāramitā , liu boluomi 六波羅蜜), with a strong emphasis on the numinous Perfection of Wisdom ( prajñāpāramitā ).

div.head: HDS 1
div.p:
The text is drawn from *Mandra[sena]’s (Mantuoluoxian 曼陀羅仙) translation of the
Śaptaśatikā­prajñā­pāramitā
(the Perfection of Wisdom in 700 Lines ), entitled Sutra on the Great Perfection of Wisdom Spoken by Mañjuśrī
( Wenshushili suoshuo mohe bore boluomi jing
文殊師利所說摩訶般若波羅蜜經; T#232). *Mandra[sena] arrived in China in 503 and was active in the Liang dynasty (502-557) in the south. He is said to have come from Funan 扶南, a country identified with part of the region now occupied by modern Cambodia. This scripture was also translated by *Saṃghabhara (Sengqiepolo 僧伽婆羅) as the Sutra on the Perfection of Wisdom Spoken by Mañjuśrī
( Wenshushili suoshuo bore boluomi jing
文殊師利所說般若波羅蜜經; T#233, 8: 732c-739c). *Saṃghabhara is also said to have come from Funan and to have worked sometimes with *Mandra[sena]. The carved passage consists of a 54 characters text on the nature of the Buddha and on how to contemplate on him, which is only contained in *Mandra[sena]’s translation (T#232, 8: 728a26-29), but not in that by *Saṃghabhara. It also occurs within a section of *Mandra[sena]’s text collected in the early eighth century translation of the Great Gem Collection Sutra (
Mahā­ratnakūṭa­sūtra , Da baoji jing 大寶積經 ; T#310, 11: 652b15-18).See Kaiyuan Era Catalog of Śākyamuni’s Teachings (Kaiyuan shijiao lu 開元釋教錄 ), T#2154, 55: 586b4-8.

The passage comes from a conversation between Śāriputra and Mañjuśrī about the nature of the Buddha. The lines that precede our passage are as follows:
Śāriputra inquired of Mañjuśrī: ‘What is named the Buddha? What is Contemplating the Buddha?’ Mañjuśrī said: ‘What is it that constitutes “I”’?’ Śāriputra said: ‘“I” is just a name and names are characterized by emptiness.” Mañjuśrī said: ‘Just so! Just so! Just as “I” is a name, so as well “Buddha” is simply a name. Names are characterized by emptiness, which is none other than bodhi. Do not seek bodhi by means of names. The attributes of bodhi cannot be put in words, cannot be explained. Why is this? Because words, explanations, and bodhi are all empty.’T#232, 8: 728a19-25: 舍利弗語文殊師利言：‘云何名佛？云何觀佛？’文殊師利言：‘云何為我？’舍利弗言：‘我者但有名字，名字相空。’文殊師利言：‘如是，如是！如我但有名字，佛亦但有名字。名字相空，即是菩提，不以名字爾求菩提。 菩提之相，無言無說。何以故？言說菩提，二俱空故。’

The Sutra on the Great Perfection of Wisdom Spoken by Mañjuśrī belongs to the
Prajñāpāramitā
group of Mahayana sutras, which promotes the Perfection of Wisdom by means of realizing emptiness ( śūnyatā , kong 空) to attain ultimate enlightenment. Perfection of Wisdom is the code of practice for a bodhisattva in his quest to save all sentient beings. The Sutra on the Great Perfection of Wisdom Spoken by Mañjuśrī
is a relatively late
Prajñāpāramitā
text, which was also influenced by tathāgatagarbha doctrine, postulating the non-duality of sentient beings and the Buddha-nature. This sutra lays particular stress on practice, promoting a meditation called “single-practice absorption” ( eka­vyūha­samādhi , yixing sanmei 一行三昧), which strongly recommends repeated and single-minded invocation of the name of a Buddha. The carving of various Buddha names on the slopes of Mount Hongding may also be related to this practice. The sutra on the Great Perfection of Wisdom Spoken by Mañjuśrī
is, in addition, one of the scriptures held in high esteem by early Chan Masters.The earliest preserved text of the Chinese Chan tradition, The Fundamental Expedient Teachings for Calming the Mind Which Attains Enlightenment ( Rudao anxinyao fangbianfamen 入道安心要方便法門 ) by Daoxin (道信, 580-651), fourth patriarch of the lineage, makes use of this sutra: see 97-100. The Great Perfection of Wisdom Spoken by Mañjuśrī is furthermore part of the Dunhuang manuscript, Pelliot chinois 4646, a collection of five texts that was identified by 64-65, as being related to Chan meditations.

The dominance of excerpts from this sutra on Mount Hongding (HDS 1, 16.2 and 22), combined with the large number of Buddha names on the one hand and allusions in the encomiums (HDS 3, 15, and 18) to contemplative achievements on the other, clearly reflects Buddhist practice of the time, which saw no incompatibility between the invocation of Buddha names and meditation.For a description of meditational and nianfo 念佛 practices by early Pure Land and Chan patriarchs and their common ground see 26-61.

The carved text of the 54 character passage of the Sutra on the Great Perfection of Wisdom Spoken by Mañjuśrī
is identical to virtually all versions that have come down to us.There is only one exception: The Dunhuang manuscript Bei 北 3492 adds the character 佛 to the phrase “云何觀佛者? 不生不滅…” to read “云何觀佛? 佛者不生不滅…”. See 78: 270b.
Exactly the same passage appears on the rock of Mount Shuiniu, on stone slabs in Cloud Dwelling Monastery (Yunju Si 雲居寺) in Fangshan 房山,See the fragments of a stone slab from cave 4, carved in the Sui or Tang dynasties, 1: 121, and stone slab no. 378 from the same cave, carved in the year 1081, 7: 371. and in the modern printed
Taishō
edition of the Buddhist canon.

div.head: HDS 2
div.p:
The passage carved here is drawn from the translation of the
*Mahāvaipulya­mahāsaṃnipāta-sūtra
by Dharmakṣema,For the translator Dharmakṣema see the study by 80-82. entitled Vast and Universal Great Collection Sutra ( Da fangdeng daji jing 大方等大集經 ). It was taken from the “Chapter on Bodhisattva Ocean-like Wisdom” (from a portion of chapter 5 in scroll 8, with chapter 5 running from scroll 8 to 11) and corresponds to T#397, 13: 50b15-18. According to tradition, Dharmakṣema was born in Central India, and traveled to the Northern Liang 北涼 capital, Guzang 姑臧, in 412, where he remained until 422. passim, and especially 257-258, following Tang Yongtong, believes that Dharma­kṣema arrived in 420. But this contradicts the evidence given in the Biographies of Eminent Monks [Gaoseng zhuan, T#2059, 50: 335c16-337b4]. After a trip to Khotan, Dharmakṣema is said to have returned to Guzang and then set off once again on a journey to the western regions during which he was killed in 433. The translation is traditionally dated to 420, though Chen Jinhua argues instead that the text dates to a period stretching from 422 to 431. 257-258.

The text of HDS 2 concerns the nature of the Six Perfections (liu boluomi 六波羅蜜)—Generosity, Precepts, Patience, Vigor, Absorption, and Wisdom—one of the basic tenets of Buddhist practice. But since the inscription was badly damaged in the late 1990s, the exact wording can no longer be established. Before that, it was transcribed only once by Kiriya Seiichi on August 20, 1995. An earlier rubbing of poor quality also exists; 84, fig. 8. it is not very reliable.

The same text passage about the nature of the Six Perfections also appears on Mount Tie in Zoucheng within the much larger passage from the Great Collection Sutra carved there. The text about the Six Perfections at Mount Tie is identical with the versions preserved in the stone-cut canon of Cloud Dwelling Monastery in Fangshan,Only the first one and a half lines of the text are preserved on the front side of stone slab no. 683 from cave 2, which was carved in the Tang dynasty, see 3: 265a. On the front side of stone slab no. 332, carved in the year 1063, from the same cave, the text is almost completely preserved, see 9: 203. and in the printed canon editions of the Song (1104-1148 and 1239), Yuan (1290) and Ming (1601) dynasties.T#397, 13: 50b15-18, and T#397, 13: 50, note 7.

div.head: HDS 5
div.p: The text of inscription HDS 5, entitled “Mahayana Sutra”, was seemingly never finished. Only a few characters were fully carved, with some additional strokes in double outlines. The inscription in the shape of a stele rests on the shell of a frontally depicted, single tortoise with uplifted head. The body of the stele was provided with a grid in double lines, and, on top, a round stele head with an inner square field for the four character title, surrounded by pairs of intertwined dragons, is still visible. Mount Hongding’s stele inscription might well have influenced the slightly later monumental stele at Mount Tie 鐵山 (Tie 1) in the city of Zoucheng 鄒城 (see Shandong, vol. 2).

The stele head carries a title reading “Mahayana Sutra” ( Moheyan jing 摩訶衍經 ). Zhang Zong argues that “Mahayana Sutra” probably refers to the Treatise on the Great Perfection of Wisdom
(
*Mahāprajñā­pāramitā­śāstra , Da zhidu lun 大智度論 )The Treatise on the Great Perfection of Wisdom is traditionally attributed to Nāgārjuna (ca. 150–250), and was supposedly translated by Kumārajīva 鳩摩羅什 (344-413). The Kuchean monk, Kumārajīva, arrived in China in the year 401, where he arguably became the single most influential translator in the history of East Asian Buddhism. His translations include versions of the Lotus Sutra and the Diamond Sutra. Chou Po-kan suggested that Kumārajīva’s disciple, Sengrui, was the actual author of the Treatise, though he does not deny the existence of a Sanskrit original, 281-327. because this sutra’s head title is Da zhidu lun in the Dunhuang manuscripts, and its end title is Mahayana Sutra . 66-68.

A passage quoted and commented upon in chapter 18 of scroll 46 of the Treatise on the Great Perfection of Wisdom
(T#1509, 25: 393c1-5) is identical to a passage introducing eighteen kinds of emptiness from the chapter “Inquiring About the Vehicle” (wensheng pin 問乘品 ) of Kumārajīva’s translation of the Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra ( Pañcaviṃśati­sāhasrikā­prajñā­pāramitā , Mohe bore boluomi jing 摩訶般若波羅蜜經, T#223, 8: 250b3-7).

The received version of the Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra in the Taishō edition differs slightly, reading “This constitutes the Mahayana of the bodhisattva. And then, Subhuti, the bodhisattva-mahāsattva also has a Mahayana...” “是為菩薩摩訶薩摩訶衍。復次，須菩提！菩薩摩訶薩復有摩訶衍...” T#223, 8: 250b2-3. The Mount Hongding text begins with the last sentence of the previous section within the source text, which it seems to present as a subject line of the text.

A thematically similar passage enumerating not eighteen, but seventeen kinds of emptiness was carved on a single rock at Mount Culai (Culai Shan 徂徠山), close to Guanghua Monastery (Guanghua Si 光化寺, see vol. 3).

div.head: HDS 6
div.p:
The passage is drawn from the Sutra Spoken by the Buddha on the Perfection of Wisdom for Humane Kings (Fo shuo renwang bore boluomi jing 佛說仁王般若波羅蜜經; T#245, 8: 825a-834a), a scripture traditionally attributed to Kumārajīva, but probably compiled in China in the 470s. This sutra was first considered spurious, and was only given canonical status in 597 by Fei Changfang 費長房 in his Record of the Three Jewels throughout Successive Generations ( Lidai sanbao ji
歷代三寶紀, T#2034, 49: 22c-127c). The sutra praises the
prajñā­pāramitā
as a means of state protection and welfare, and was so used by Emperor Daizong 代宗 (r. 762-779) of the Tang dynasty, who initiated a “Humane Kings” assembly and ordered a second translation of the sutra in 765 by the famous monk Amoghavajra (Bukong 不空, 705-774). 74-78. On the basis of the second translation, state-protecting rituals later became popular in Korea and Japan. Apart from its political relevance, the passage, at Mount Hongding, chosen from this sutra has a Daoist flavor,According to 85, that was the reason why the passage as carved at Mount Hongding is only found in the fifth century translation, but was omitted by Amoghavajra in his “translation”, which was actually just a second edition of the text. invoking the magical qualities ascribed to the
prajñāpāramitā
. The Perfection of Wisdom is likened to a series of powerful talismanic objects, which is notable, not simply because it does so—many texts employ similar vivid metaphors—but because some of these objects are distinctively Chinese.On later Chinese talsimanic objects see .

A modern collated edition of the received version of this passage is located at T#245, 8: 832c23-26. The passage does not include King Prasenajit (Posini wang 波斯匿王) seen in HDS 6, but simply begins “Great King” (dawang 大王), since the king’s name has been introduced before at T#245, 8: 832b20. The wording of the carved passage otherwise follows the passage in the
Taishō
edition, and those of a few manuscripts from the fifth century that have come down to us. 79, note 39, mentions fragmentary Dunhuang manuscripts of the scripture: S. 124, S. 3472, S. 4528, S. 7242, Bei 北 3481-3.

div.head: HDS 16.2 and HDS 22
div.p: HDS 16.2 and HDS 22 both present the same 98 character passage from the Sutra on the Great Perfection of Wisdom Spoken by Mañjuśrī (T#232, 8: 726a-732c) on the nature of the Perfection of Wisdom. This passage is drawn from the *Mandra­[sena] (Mantuoluoxian 曼陀羅仙, fl. 503) translation of the Śaptaśatikā­prajñā­pāramitā-sūtra (the Perfection of Wisdom in 700 Lines). As mentioned above (HDS 1), *Mandra­[sena] worked with *Saṃgha­bhara (Sengqiepolo 僧伽婆羅; b. 460), who also translated the Śapta­śatikā­prajñā­pāramitā­sūtra (T#233, 8: 732c-739c). In the current collated Taishō edition, a passage that is identical with the carved text is found in Saṃgabhara’s translation (T#233, 8: 738a19-24), but it differs somewhat from the same section in *Mandra[sena]’s translation. However, a careful examination of the transmission of the text shows that this passage, found in the carved text as well as in Saṃgabhara’s translation, is indeed a later interpolation unknown to the Taishō edition.The interpolation was first noticed by Luo Zhao during his stay at Heidelberg University in 2004.

Versions of both translations, those by *Mandra[sena] and by Saṃgabhara, survive in the carved canon of Cloud Dwelling Monastery at Fangshan near Beijing. As the Fangshan canon shows, formulations of *Saṃgabhara’s original translation of this passage display strong differences from the carved passage on Mount Hongding. On the other hand, *Mandra[sena]’s translation differs from the carved passage by only one character.

The earliest Fangshan version of the 98 characters passage is found on stone slabs in cave 4 of Stone Sutra Mountain 石經山. Carved during the Sui or Tang dynasties, these slabs preserve *Mandra[sena]’s translation. 1: 125. Stone slabs in cave 4 carved during the Liao 遼 and Jin 金 dynasties also carry the translation by “the Tripitaka Master from Funan, *Mandra[sena]”, as well as that by “the Tripitaka Master from Funan, *Saṃgha­bhara”. 30: 233. *Saṃghabhara’s original version of the passage in question appears on stone slab no. 374, starting at column 13, counting from the left. 7: 375. It is quite different from the corresponding passage by *Man­dra­[sena]. This leads us to conclude that at some point the relevant passage in *Mandra­[sena]’s translation was substituted for the original passage in *Saṃghabhara’s translation, which is preserved also in Dunhuang manuscripts S. 576 and Bei 北 3497.

In addition, Dunhuang manuscript S. 2653 is also identical with the rock cut Shandong version. Later on, the text of this passage was amended, probably to make the meaning clearer.The text version of stone slab no. 378, from cave 2 at Fangshan, was carved in 1081, 7: 372. It exhibits such amendments: to “非行非不行處悉入一乘”, it adds an additional 處 so that the passage reads “非行處非不行處悉入一乘”, perhaps to smooth the reading of the phrase. This amendment is lost later on, when printed editions of the Buddhist canon, including the modern
Taishō
edition, keep the character 處, but drop the original 行. These editions also add two more characters, changing 何故 to 何以故, and 如 to 猶如. Unlike the amendments, which slightly lengthen the text, the versions preserved in manuscripts tend to shorten the text by employing contractions.Dunhuang manuscripts S. 1908 and Bei 北 3492 reduce 無邊無際 to 无邊際, and 無名無相 to 无名相.

Although the wording of inscription HDS 16.2 on the northern slope of Mount Hongding is the same as that of HDS 22 on the southern slope, both inscriptions differ in their writing of the character 無: HDS 16.2 uses the abbreviated character 无 throughout, while HDS 22 prefers 無 in all but one instance. Similarily, at Mount Yi near Zoucheng this passage was also carved twice: At Bewitching Fairy Cave (Yaojing Dong 妖精洞), the character 无 is used, and at Five Blossoms Peak (Wuhua Feng 五華峯) the character 無 (see Shandong, vol. 2).

The ten historical inscriptions can be divided into two groups, those pertaining to the monk donor and calligrapher Seng’an Daoyi, and those relating to the monk donor Fahong. In both groups are encomiums (HDS 3 for Seng’an Daoyi, with its title HDS 4, and HDS 18 for Fahong). In addition we find an unusual stele text for Seng’an Daoyi (HDS 15), a colophon by him (HDS 7), and possibly a postscript to his writings (HDS 14). Both monks left signatures (Seng’an Daoyi: HDS 16.1; Fahong: HDS 21.2, HDS 23.2, and, most likely, HDS 9.16).

div.head: HDS 3
div.p: One of the fascinating and remarkable features of the inscriptions on Mount Hongding is the presence of the two encomiums. There are no other encomiums on the polished cliffs.

The “Encomium to Seng’an Daoyi” (HDS 3) praises the spiritual achievements of this monk. According to the conventions of literary genres, the first sentence of the encomium leads the reader to expect a biography, and the language is similar to that used in epitaphs of eminent monks. Unlike an epitaph, however, this encomium gives no information about Seng’an Daoyi’s life, but reflects first of all soteriological needs, when it describes him as “a man of the neighborhood of Empty Immensity within the village of Vast Enormity”. The encomium is comparable to that to Fahong on the southern slope (HDS 18).

The two encomiums follow the same general scheme, but are rather different from the more general biographies and epitaphs of eminent monks. Both might be seen as constituting a separate genre of monastic encomium, one which only generally resembles traditional biographical genres.

Seng’an’s encomium is one of three inscriptions making use of the Buddhist calendar’s dating in years after the Buddha’s demise. However, here the exact number of years after the Buddha’s nirvana is illegible.

div.head: HDS 4
div.p: In HDS 4 Seng’an Daoyi’s name in four oversize characters is inserted into a frame in the shape of a stele with a pointed head. It functions like a title to the adjacent encomium, HDS 3. The relationship between the two calligraphies is comparable to that between the two parts of a square tomb inscription (muzhiming 墓誌銘).

The large characters on the cover stone of a tomb inscription include the name of the deceased, while his biography is carved in small characters on the stone below. This type of tomb inscription had evolved from the Western Han dynasty, but it became widespread only under the Northern dynasties. 40.

div.head: HDS 7
div.p: This colophon identifies the renunciant Seng’an Daoyi as the calligrapher of the giant four-character inscription “Buddha King of Great Emptiness” (HDS 8) to the right. It explicitly states that “the Great Renunciant Seng’an Daoyi wrote and carved: Buddha King of Great Emptiness, seven…, wrote sutras…”, and thereby refers to several inscriptions that Seng’an personally inscribed on the rock. The meaning of the word “seven” probably refers to seven places where the name “Buddha King of Great Emptiness” was carved.

There are altogether six places on Mount Hongding with larger or smaller “Buddha King of Great Emptiness” inscriptions, including HDS 8. At least two of them (HDS 21.1 and 23.1) were donated not by Seng’an, but by Fahong. But in neighboring Pingyin County 平陰縣, more “Buddha King of Great Emptiness” inscriptions were discovered on Mount Ergu 二鼓山, Mount Yuncui 雲翠山, Mount Tianchi 天池山 and East Mount Shuyuan 書院東山. All these Buddha names have been discussed as possible works by Seng’an. 14; 96.

The colophon is one of the three historical inscriptions at Mount Hongding that refer to the Buddhist calendar. It gives “1,623 years after Śākya­muni [entered into nirvana] under the twin trees”. According to Zhang Zong, 12-13. this date corresponds to the year 556, based on the years after the beginning of the End of the Law as calculated by Huisi (515-577).

div.head: HDS 9.16
div.p:
A monk Fahong 法鴻 has signed here as donor for the large group of Buddha names on the northern slope (HDS 9.1-9.15). In all likelihood, this person is identical with monk Fahong 法洪
268 and 176. who donated two Buddha names on the southern slope (HDS 21.2 and 23.2), and who is venerated in an encomium (HDS 18).

div.head: HDS 14
div.p: The four characters reading “Stele of Wind Entrance” have to be seen in close relation to neighboring inscriptions HDS 15 and HDS 16. These might be a form of postscript to these Seng’an Daoyi related inscriptions, perhaps carved by some of his followers. 59.

div.head: HDS 15
div.p: This inscription entitled “Stele of Sire Serenity” presents a word play on Seng’an Daoyi’s four character name. It makes use of each character’s meaning, first establishing subtle relations between them, and finally reducing them to what may be called the very essence of Buddhist practice: Serenity and Unity. This extraordinary inscription is dated “1,620 years after [Śākyamuni entered into nirvana under] the twin trees”, and is thus three years earlier than HDS 7. According to Huisi’s calculation of the End of the Law, this date should correspond to the year 553.

It is unlikely that Seng’an himself composed and wrote the inscription for his honorary stele, and some authors therefore believe it to be a work by his followers. 238-239 and 326-329. Yet it has also been suggested that the monk might have been asked by his disciples to write the sophisticated and cryptic text with his own hand. 279.

div.head: HDS 16.1
div.p:
This signature reading “Seng’an Daoyi” is carved next to the sutra passage of 98 characters taken from the Sutra on the Great Perfection of Wisdom Spoken by Mañjuśrī
(HDS 16.2). It designates Seng’an either as calligrapher of the sutra passage, or as donor, or even both.

div.head: HDS 18
div.p: Like HDS 3, the “Encomium to Seng’an Daoyi”, the text of the encomium for Fahong, is not included in any traditional collection. Again, the only parallels for this monastic encomium are the traditional biographical genres, as in HDS 3. The first sentence leads one to expect a biography, yet what follows is more a panegyric to Fahong’s talents and his mastery of doctrines than a biography in the usual sense. By contrast with the encomium for Seng’an Daoyi, the one for Fahong uses the regular calendar for dating the inscription to the third year of the he­qing era of the Northern Qi dynasty (564).

div.head: HDS 21.2
div.p: Fahong seems to have donated not only the name Buddha King of Great Emptiness (HDS 21.1) right above this inscription, but also the sutra passage of HDS 22 to the right, for he is addressed as “Donor of Sutras”.

div.head: HDS 23.2
div.p: The same monk Fahong, who is praised in the encomium HDS 18 and named as donor in HDS 21.2, appears here as donor of the name “Buddha King of Great Emptiness” (HDS 23.1).

div.head: Layout
div.p: Five types of layout can be identified.

list.item: Rectangular layout without frame. Except for HDS 8, all Buddha names written in one vertical column belong in this group (HDS 9.1-9.15, 10-13, 17, 19, 20, 21.1, 23.1), as does the short single column “Stele of Wind Entrance” (HDS 14). Texts with more than one column, namely the sutra passages HDS 1, 2, 6, and 16.2, the encomium HDS 3, and the colophon HDS 7 also take this form. In the sutra passages and, indeed, in all texts with several columns carved at Mount Hongding, the characters have been arranged in horizontal rows (exception: last column of HDS 3). In HDS 6, this invisible grid has been calculated in such a way that the two characters, yi ming 亦名, mostly stand next to each other horizontally. In the largely destroyed HDS 2, the entire fifth row was probably filled by the character ming 名.
Double frame. Only one Buddha name is encased in a double frame, namely the giant HDS 8.

Grid. In the “Encomium to Fahong”, HDS 18, the grid of the multi-column inscriptions has been made visible explicitly by carving thin lines (the “Encomium to Seng’an Daoyi”, HDS 3, has no such grid lines). In the sutra passage HDS 22, only the vertical columns are separated by double grid lines, but not the horizontal rows.

Stele shape. Three inscriptions are laid out like a stele. While Seng’an Daoyi’s name in HDS 4 is simply encased by outlines in the shape of a stele, the contours of a full-fledged stele have been carved onto the rock for the sutra passage HDS 5. HDS 15 is surmounted by a separate field for the stele title in four characters that explicitly calls the text a “stele” (bei 碑). The one column inscription HDS 14 equally calls itself a stele, yet no trace of a stele shape is visible here.

Combination of two inscriptions. In several instances two inscriptions are combined in a single layout. Seng’an Daoyi’s name, HDS 4, relates to his encomium, HDS 3, in the manner of the cover stone of a tomb inscription to the inscription below it. HDS 7 is a colophon to the Buddha name, HDS 8, adjacent on its right. Signatures can be placed to the right, to the left, or below the text to which they refer. The signature, HDS 9.16, refers to the 15 Buddha names 9.1- 9.15 on its right. HDS 21.2 is a signature to the Buddha name on its left, and HDS 16.1 a signature to the sutra text on its left. HDS 23.2. is the signature to the Buddha name 23.1 above it, and the signature in HDS 13 is equally placed beneath the Buddha name.

div.head: Carving Techniques
div.p: Having examined the carving methods of Mount Hongding’s stone sutras through a transverse section analysis of its strokes, we conclude that the techniques employed fall into five categories:

list.item: U-shaped carving: the carver chisels in one movement along the middle of the stroke. Convenient and fast, this technique is suitable for the carving of strokes 0.2-0.4 cm wide. The lines of the stele frame for the Mahayana Sutra on Mount Hongding (HDS 5) are carved by this method.

V-shaped carving: the carver chisels slanting lines from both sides of a stroke, so that the lines intersect and the stroke naturally takes shape. Convenient and fast, this technique is suitable for the carving of strokes 0.7-4.0 cm wide. 90 % of Mount Hongding’s inscriptions are carved by this method.

Double-outline carving: the carver chisels only the outline on either sides of a stroke, so that the interior of the stroke remains untouched. This technique is suitable for the carving of strokes more than 4 centimeters wide. The “Buddha King of Great Emptiness” inscriptions on the southern slope of Mount Hongding (HDS 19, 20, 21.1, and 23.1) are carved by this method.

Rectangular U-shaped carving: the carver chisels vertical lines on both sides of a stroke. This technique is suitable for strokes more than 10 cm wide. Mount Hongding’s “Buddha King of Great Emptiness” (HDS 8) and “Buddha Great Mountain Cliff” (HDS 12) are carved by this method (fig. ).

“Flying white” carving: the carver first chisels the double outlines of a stroke, and then adds several decorative lines within the stroke to create an effect, similar to “flying white” calligraphy. The giant “Buddha King of Great Emptiness” inscription (HDS 8) on the northern slope of Mount Hongding is carved by this method.

div.head: Style of Calligraphy
div.p: Typologically speaking, that is, given the number and position of strokes in a character, the inscriptions belong to the late phase of the clerical script (lishu 隸書). This type had its own development in the north where, unlike in the south, it turned into the standard script (kaishu 楷書) only gradually. This amalgam created during the Northern dynasties has sometimes been called kaili 楷隸 and sometimes likai 隸楷. It retains the typological features of the clerical script: the radical silk, si 糸, is written with two superimposed triangles (HDS 9.2, 9.15); we find a square on the left side of the yi 以 (HDS 16.2, 10/2; HDS 22, 7/8); hooks in the zhou 洲 instead of dots (HDS 16.2, 4/10), and the composition of the chu 處 remains rather different from the standard form (HDS 16.2, 8/6).

Stylistically, the clerical script heritage makes itself felt in conspicuous soft na 捺 strokes (the last strokes in characters drawn out diagonally toward the lower right), and in overall squat proportions (HDS 16.2). However, these two stylistic features are most conspicuous in the Buddha names and sutra passages and less so in the two encomiums (HDS 3 and 18), the stele for Seng’an Daoyi (HDS 15) and in his colophon (HDS 7). The calligraphers freely use deviant forms and abbreviated characters, adding to the picture of typological and stylistic fuzziness.

The four title characters of the “Stele of Sire Serenity” (HDS 15) are written in seal script. This script type was commonly used in stele heads (bei’e 碑額) and on tomb stones during the Northern dynasties.For stele heads in seal script see 46-50. In the sixth century, a rustic style with decorative, sometimes fancy strokes, as on the Stele for Gao Zhen 高貞碑 of 523,Stone kept in the Shandong Stone Carving Art Museum in Jinan. and on the stele, which Lou Rui 婁睿 had made around 560, plates 9 and 11. developed into a smoother and more angular style, as on the Stele for Meng Xianda 孟顯達碑 of 600.Stone in the Xi’an Beilin Museum. Illustration of rubbing in pl. 35. The seal script title of HDS 15 is already closer to the latter.

According to character size and depth of carving, the inscriptions can be divided into three groups that, by and large, coincide with the content of the texts. Buddha names are written in large characters, some more than a meter in height. Exceptionally large is HDS 8; its first character da 大 measures 4 meters in width. The large characters are deeply carved and the strokes are often given a special structure. The three dimensional effect of superimposed strokes and of flying white (HDS 8, 1/1-4; HDS 9.1, 1/1; HDS 9.2, 1/1) is only used for the Buddha names. They can have double contour carving. The name Seng’an Daoyi is also written in these large characters (HDS 4); apparently there was no lack of self confidence on the part of this monk.

Sutra texts are written with medium size characters in imaginary squares measuring about 20 centimeters in height and width. The carving is shallower than that of the Buddha names. The smallest characters tend to be those of historical inscriptions, including the signatures. They are often barely higher than 10 centimeters. The carving is shallow, and consequently more effaced and harder to decipher. As a result, these inscriptions are especially difficult to reconstruct because, unlike the Buddha names and the sutra passages, such texts with historical content have not been transmitted in other manuscripts or printed versions with which they might be compared.
